coredata.dc:title: A viscosity-dependent carbon dots with anti-VEGF properties for monitoring and promoting apoptosis in cancerous cell
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Sun, Xiaodong
Ma, Tengchuang
Zhang, Yan
Jiang, Yingxia
Wang, Ruijie
Ma, Cong
Wei, Junfu
Chen, Li
Cui, Yali
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) as a fluorescent nanomaterial plays an important role in bioimaging and light-emitting devices, and its multifunctionalization will be a promising evolution. Here, by a freely rotatable single bond to connect materials with complementary functions together, a dual-function aggregation-induced emission (AIE) CDs (V-CDs) is obtained to visually monitor the dead of cancerous cells and inhibit the production of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). The fluorescence of V-CDs will change between blue and green as the changes of environment viscosity based on the restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) effect. For another, the VEGF content decreased by about 3 times compared with the blank group after V-CDs was introduced. The co-administration group shows the best anti-cancer effect among the rest of control groups after Taxol was involved, and the same result are achieved in vitro and vivo. The performances of V-CDs make it possible to realize the potential of visualizing anti-cancer process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
AIE
Viscosity
VEGF pathway
Combination anticancer

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Inhibition of VEGF by V-CDs in vitro
The combination anticancer of V-CDs and taxol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a. TEM of pre-CDs (insert up: HRTEM; down: particles size distribution of pre-CDs); b. TEM of V-CDs (insert up: HRTEM; down: particles size distribution of V-CDs); c. XRD pattern of V-CDs; d. The full XPS spectrum of V-CDs; c–f. Curve of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S2p.

a. UV–vis spectrum of HBT, pre-CDs and V-CDs (Water); b. FT-IR of HBT, pre-CDs and V-CDs; c. Raman spectra of HBT, pre-CDs and V-CDs; d. 1H NMR of pre-CDs and V-CDs; d. 1H NMR of pre-CDs and V-CDs; e. Suggested plausible V-CDs structure model.

a. The excitation and emission spectra of HBT in ethanol and glycerol; b. Emission spectra of pre-CDs in ethanol; c. Emission spectra of V-CDs in ethanol; d. Emission spectra of V-CDs in Glycerol; e. V-CDs selectivity in different solutions; f. Fluorescence spectra of V-CDs at pH 3–11 (λex = 370 nm); g. Fluorescence emission of V-CDs spectra in glycerol (0–99%) solutions (λex = 370 nm); h. chromaticity coordinate of g (Insert: Fluorescent photographs).

a. Emission spectra of HBT, pre-CDs and V-CDs in ethanol and glycerol; b UV–vis spectra of HBT-CHO, HBT and V-CDs in ethanol; c. UV–vis spectra of HBT-CHO, HBT and V-CDs in glycerol; d. Fluorescence photos of the gel swelling process with time; e. Fluorescence spectra of gels (λex = 370 nm); f. Fluorescence photos and dimensional changes of the gels; g. Schematic diagram of fluorescence mechanism of V-CDs.

a. Cells fluorescence change with apoptosis (collected by green channel); b. Spatial structure model of ethyl-HBT; c. a docking model of VEGFR2 to an ethyl-HBT; d. Representative fluorescence images of VEGF expression by V-CDs after 24 h (collected by blue channel, red channel, and merge image); e. The corresponding expression of VEGF with the treatment of V-CDs (n = 3). P value was counted by Student's ttest. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a. Representative fluorescence images of caspase-3 expression by synergistic treatment of V-CDs and Taxol (collected by blue channel, green channel, and merge image); b. Intensity expression of caspase-3 in control, V-CDs, Taxol and V-CDs + Taxol (100 μg/mL, n = 3); c. Western blotting photo of control, V-CDs, Taxol and V-CDs + Taxol; d. Intensity of Western blotting. P value was counted by Student's ttest. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The assumed mechanism and achieved effect of V-CDs in anticancer. Up: The design idea of this work and the visual anti-tumor process of V-CDs and Taxol; Down: Simplified VEGF pathway [36,37] and the possible role of V-CDs (Insert: docking model, by Autodock & Pymol).

head.ce:title: A viscosity-dependent carbon dots with anti-VEGF properties for monitoring and promoting apoptosis in cancerous cell

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes/National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes/National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes/National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nuclear Medicine, Harbin Medical University Cancer Hospital, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150081, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nuclear Medicine, Harbin Medical University Cancer Hospital, Harbin, Heilongjiang, 150081, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: F. Yan, X. Sun, and T. Ma contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a fluorescent nanomaterial plays an important role in bioimaging and light-emitting devices, and its multifunctionalization will be a promising evolution. Here, by a freely rotatable single bond to connect materials with complementary functions together, a dual-function aggregation-induced emission (AIE) CDs (V-CDs) is obtained to visually monitor the dead of cancerous cells and inhibit the production of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). The fluorescence of V-CDs will change between blue and green as the changes of environment viscosity based on the restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) effect. For another, the VEGF content decreased by about 3 times compared with the blank group after V-CDs was introduced. The co-administration group shows the best anti-cancer effect among the rest of control groups after Taxol was involved, and the same result are achieved in vitro and vivo. The performances of V-CDs make it possible to realize the potential of visualizing anti-cancer process.

ce:section.ce:para: Unlimited proliferation, invade, and metastasize are three characteristics of cancerous cells, which causes incomplete treatment that can be attributed to a key factor of tumor vascular disorder and excessive growth, and existing means still cannot effectively control this situation [1–4]. Blocking VEGF pathway is a feasible anti-cancer therapeutic approach that cuts off the supply of nutrients by inhibiting the production of blood vessels around the tumor tissue to prevent the spread and metastasis of cancerous tissues [5–8]. The main advantages of tumor vascular severing therapy include universal applicability, less drug resistance, low side effects, and strong anti-cancer ability, all of which can be combined with chemotherapy drugs to achieve rapid and reliable apoptosis of cancerous cells [9–15].

AIE is an abnormal fluorescence phenomenon. The general comprehension of AIE is the fluorescence enhancement caused by the reduced energy of the non-radiative transition due to the aggregation of molecules [16]. Since the discovery of the AIE phenomenon, researchers have utilized the special fluorescent properties in many applications fields including analytical detection, bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, room temperature phosphorescence, photothermal therapy, etc. [17,18] However, most organic AIE molecules with multi-aromatic structures are hardly soluble in aqueous solution and forced to exhibit AIE process, which makes the AIE nature difficult to apply in a multi-watery environment, like organism [19]. A straightforward method is to modify AIE luminogen (AIEgen) onto materials with good water solubility. As a zero-dimensional nanomaterial, CDs exhibit excellent properties in fluorescence and biocompatibility [20]. In the continuous research on it, people have expanded its applications in display, analysis detection, bioimaging, disease treatment, etc. [21–23] The surface of the CDs can be enriched with hydrophilic groups such as amino groups, carboxyl groups, and hydroxyl groups through specific synthesis methods [24,25]. The size distribution (<10 nm) also makes it show good biocompatibility [26]. However, unlike AIEgen, CDs often have an aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect [27]. Therefore, the combination of these two materials with opposite functions is of great significance for the discovery of new materials.

Finding small molecules with AIE properties and anti-VEGF activity is a logical idea that is used to visualize the anti-cancer process, and hereby a small molecule with a distinct AIE effect and a thiazole structure capable of binding to vascular endothelial growth factor receptor 2 (VEGFR2) named 2-(2′-hydroxyphenyl)benzothiazole (HBT) catches our attention [28–30]. According to our experience of CDs modification [31–33], we developed a special method to synthesize V-CDs to ensure that the properties of CDs and HBT can be used at the same time. In this article, a lower temperature solvothermal condition is carried out in the process of synthesizing pre-CDs. It has a lower degree of carbonization compared to the previous carbon synthesis process, which is advantageous for further modification. HBT is linked to pre-CDs by constructing a Schiff base structure, and then the imine is restored by sodium borohydride to obtain V-CDs (Scheme S1). The indispensable reduction process is carried out for several intentions: it improves the stability of the entire structure [34]; it makes the AIE effect more obvious due to the generation of a single bond [35]; and it allows HBT molecules to perform better physiological functions because of the flexible single bond. The combination of pre-CDs and HBT makes the V-CDs emit fluorescence of pre-CDs at low viscosity and show AIE fluorescence at high viscosity, a change of blue to green, which conforms to the phenomenon both in gel swelling experiments and cancerous cells apoptosis experiments. In immunofluorescence and Western blot experiments, the results indicate that V-CDs can inhibit the production of VEGF in cells and effectively kill cancerous cells synergistic treatment with Taxol. Compared with other nanomaterials (Table S1), the characteristics of V-CDs enable it to exert the desired effect that promotes cancerous cells apoptosis and shows the apoptosis process (Scheme 1 ).

The pre-CDs is synthesized according the reported method with further modification to make the CDs modify more easily [33]. In detail, methanol (25 mL) as solvent, citric acid (0.10 g, 0.52 mmol) and diethylenetriamine (0.56 mL, 5.20 mmol) were added to the methanol. Ultrasonic (30 min) was used to make the compound to pre-polymerize, then the solution was transferred to the hydrothermal reactor to continue react 8 h under 110 °C to get crude CDs. It was purified through dialysis bag (500 Da) against water 48 h then dried under vacuum to obtain the orange yellow viscous liquid (0.52 g, pre-CDs) stored at 4 °C in the dark for further use.

HBT-CHO is synthesized by follow literature [35], 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6, δ) 12.99 (s, 1H, OH), 10.37 (s, 1H, CHO), 8.38 (d, J = 7.7 Hz, 1H, Ar H), 8.19 (dd, J = 14.3, 8.0 Hz, 1H, Ar H), 8.12 (t, J = 7.1 Hz, 1H, Ar H), 7.93 (t, J = 7.6 Hz, 1H, Ar H), 7.60 (dd, J = 13.2, 5.4 Hz, 1H, Ar H), 7.56–7.44 (m, 1H, Ar H), 7.24 (dd, J = 17.1, 8.7 Hz, 1H, Ar H); 13C NMR (101 MHz, DMSO‑d 6, δ) 191.75 (s), 161.48 (s), 159.49 (s), 151.40 (s), 133.89 (s), 132.82 (s), 132.23 (s), 129.23 (s), 126.99 (s), 125.69 (s), 123.91 (s), 122.85 (s), 122.52 (s), 118.08 (s). First step, HBT-CHO and pre-CDs are linked together through the formation of Schiff base. Specifically, methanol (30 mL) as reaction solvent in three-neck flask, HBT-CHO (0.30 g, 1.18 mmol) and pre-CDs (0.40 g) were added in the flask at room temperature for 3 h monitored by TLC. Second step, sodium borohydride (0.90 g, 0.024 mol) was added into the system under 5 °C and then react at room temperature for 2 h. The solvent was evaporated after the reaction is completed by TLC. Then, V-CDs was purified by silica gel column [acetic acid/Methanol/EA, (1/1/2, V/V/V)] to obtained a khaki solid (0.31 g).

0.33 g acrylic acid of 60% neutralization degree was adding to a three-necked flask with 100 mL distilled water under 50 °C, then sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (0.21 g) were slowly added to the system with stirring the solution. After sodium carboxymethyl cellulose completely dissolved, the acrylamide (2.08 g, 0.029 mol) was added into solution, then ammonium persulphate (0.0067 g, 0.029 mmol) was added under N2 for 2 h. Methylene-Bis-Acrylamide (0.042 g, 2.93 mmol) and V-CDs mother liquid (2 mg/mL, 10 mL) were added into and stir for 1 h to get the solution of high absorbent resin. The high absorbent resin was dried at 90 °C and storage at 25 °C in the dark for further use.

Human breast carcinoma cell lines (MDA-MB-231) were cultured according to the method described below. All cells were inoculated in a confocal culture dish for 24 h for dark field scattering imaging test. Then 50 μg/mL of the nanomaterial was incubated with the cells. After 12 h of culture, the cells were washed with PBS three times. The light scattering images were obtained by using a dark field condenser inverted phase fluorescence microscope. For the cytotoxicity test, MDA-MB-231 cells were planted on 96-well plates. MTT assay was used to detect the toxicity of different concentration nanomaterials after incubation with different cells for 12 h and 24 h.

Cells were cultivated with 0.1% Triton X-100 for 5 min and 4% paraformaldehyde for 20 min. The samples were washed and blocked with 5% bovine serum albumin in PBS. Then the samples were incubated with primary antibodies against ZO-1 (1:50), V-CDs, and Taxol at 4 °C overnight, followed by incubation with fluorescence-labeled rabbit secondary antibody (1:100) at room temperature for 1 h. The nuclei were stained with DAPI for 10 min before observation.

Cells were planted on a confocal dish (Coverglass Bottom Dish) at the density of 5 × 104 cells for 24 h. The control group was the wells without any treatment. After treatment for 3 h, the cells were fixed by paraformaldehyde and then washed with PBS. Then a blocking buffer was added and kept at room temperature for 1 h. Afterward, the cells and anti-protein mouse monoclonal antibody (dilution 1:1000) were incubated together at 4 °C overnight. The next day, the sheep antimouse secondary antibody (dilution 1:1000) was incubated at room temperature for 1 h. The nuclei were stained with DAPI for 10 min before observation.

The Western blotting were performed in triplicate. Cell lysates were collected by RIPA protein extraction buffer with protease inhibitor cocktail. 10% sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide electrophoresis gel was used to separate Cell lysates then the cells were transferred onto a polyvinylidene fluoride membrane. The membrane was incubated with primary antibodies at 4 °C overnight. Horseradish peroxidase-conjugated anti-mouse or anti-rabbit secondary antibody was added separately.

A 1 × 106 MDA-MB-231 cell suspension in 100 μL of PBS was inoculated in the left behind legs of female BALB/c nude mice. The tumor-bearing mice were divided into four groups (six mice each group) when the tumor was about 100 mm3: (a) control, (b) V-CDs, (c) Taxol, (d) V-CDs + Taxol. First, all tumor-bearing mice of groups were administrated nanomaterials via the vein. Mouse weight and tumor growth were measured after administration for 20 days. The length and width of tumor were gauged through a Vernier caliper every day. When the whole experiment finished, all mice were killed according to the protocols of the Harbin Medical University Cancer Hospital. The tumor was removed and weighed to calculate the therapy efficacy among five groups.

All reagents were purchased from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. Twice-distilled water was used throughout all experiments. All reagents including: 2-Aminothiophenol, salicylaldehyde, ethanol, hydrogen peroxide, various anion solution such as hypochlorite, trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), hexamethylenetramine (HMTA), methylene chloride (DCM), citric acid, diethylenetriamine, sodium borohydride, acrylic acid, carboxymethylcellulose sodium (CMC), acrylamide, ammonium persulphate, methylene-bis-acrylamide. All reagents were purchased from Tianjin, China. All chemicals were used without further purification. 2-aminobenzenethiol Analytical Reagent (AR) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., LTD. Trifluoroacetic Acid (TFA) and methylene-Bis-Acrylamide were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Hexamethylenetetramine, Citric acid, acrylamide, ammonium persulphate and Diethylenetriamine were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., LTD. Solvents and test agents were purchased from Tianjin fengchuan chemical reagent technology Co., LTD. The silica gel of thin layer chromatography (TLC) and column chromatography (200–300 mesh) was purchased from Qingdao Ocean Chemical Co., LTD.

All biologically relevant experiments are done by Harbin Medical University. Human breast cancerous cells MDA-MB-231 were acquired from Harbin Medical University Cancer Hospital. MTT assay and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were obtained from Harbin Medical University Laboratories. Roswell Park Memorial Institute-1640 (RPMI-1640), Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM), and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Gibco (Shanghai, China). All antibodies were purchased from the Abcam Company. The female BALB/c mice were supplied by Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). RIPA protein extraction buffer were purchased from Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology (Beijing, China). polyvinylidene fluoride membrane were purchased from EMD Millipore Billerica, MA, USA. protease inhibitor cocktail was purchased from Roche, Basel, Switzerland. The SuperSignal kit was purchased from Pierce, Rockford, IL, USA.

The morphology of CDs was observed on a Talos F200X High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope with FEG. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) were obtained by an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer. XRD were obtained by a Bruker D8advance. IR spectra were obtained by a TENSOR37 FT-IR (KBr disks). Raman spectra were obtained by a Laser Confocal Micro-Raman Spectroscopy (LabRAM Aramis). NMR spectra were measured with a Bruker Advance III HD 400 MHz spectrometer, d-DMSO (TMS as an internal standard) as solvent. The UV–vis spectra were measured by Purkinje General TU-1901 UV–vis spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra were measured by F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The pH measurements were measured by a Mettler Toledo pH meter. All optical measurements were carried out through Standard quartz cuvettes of 1 cm path length. All cells were observed by the Leica SP5 confocal microscopy. The WB bands were analyzed using the ChemiDocTM MP Imaging System (Bio-Rad Laboratories, Inc., Hercules, CA, USA).

The V-CDs were characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM), XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1a and b, the TEM image of pre-CDs indicates the size distributions between 5 nm and 8 nm with average diameter about 5.6 nm. The TEM image of V-CDs shows the size distributions between 6 nm and 12 nm with average diameter about 8.7 nm. The increasing diameter indicates the change in morphology of pre-CDs and implies the completion of HBT modification. As shown in HRTEM (insert of Fig. 1a and b), a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm corresponding to (002) facet of graphite indicates that these two CDs contain graphite-like structure and the process of modification does not change the characteristic [38]. In addition, the XRD pattern displays the characteristic peak at 25.9° attributed to 0.34 nm interlayer spacing (Fig. 1c), which indicated a similar conclusion with HRTEM [39]. The full XPS spectrum shows four peaks at 285.08 eV, 399.08 eV, 531.08 eV and 164.08 eV, implying that the V-CDs included C, N, O, and S elements (Fig. 1d). The atomic ratios were calculated to 77.47%, 10.16%, 8.74%, and 2.13%, respectively. The curve of C 1s is fitted into four peaks including C-C/C=C bonds (284.78 eV), CN bonds (285.46 eV), C-S/C-O bonds (286.12 eV), and C=C/C=N bonds (288.13 eV). The curve of N 1 s is fitted into four peaks including pyridinic N (399.20 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.67 eV). The curve of S2p is fitted into four peaks including ph-S-C (S2p3/2 163.94 eV) and ph-S-C (S2p1/2 165.08 eV) [40] (Fig. 1, e–h).

To demonstrate that HBT has been modified on the surface of pre-CDs, a series of characterizations were performed. In UV–vis shown in Fig. 2a, the peak at 340 nm in pre-CDs is due to the n-π⁎ transition corresponding to amino and carboxyl groups present on the surface of the CDs. HBT has absorption peaks at 290 nm and 320 nm, which belongs to π-π⁎ (ph) transition and n-π⁎ (N, S) transition, respectively. The π-π⁎ transition of HBT and the n-π⁎ transition of pre-CDs can be observed in the UV–vis spectrum of V-CDs [41]. UV–vis spectra indicate that aromatic compounds are introduced in pre-CDs. In order to better illustrate the changes of functional groups on the surface of pre-CDs, FT-IR spectra for pre-CDs, HBT-CHO and V-CDs are measured as shown in Fig. 2b. V-CDs contains a wide vibration band from 3500 to 3200 cm−1 belonging to the -NH2 and -OH stretching of pre-CDs. The sp3 hybrid CH stretching vibration peak from 2900 to 3000 cm−1 are also observed. The characteristic absorption peak in the fingerprint region of the amide bond (CO, about 1640 cm−1), sp2 hybrid carbon (CC, about 1580 cm−1) and carbon‑oxygen (CO, about 1000 cm−1) stretching vibration are observed. Characteristic CS bond appears at 650 cm−1 in V-CDs [42]. FT-IR spectra show that the HBT molecule had been modified to pre-CDs. To further demonstrate the structural changes of the two CDs, Raman spectra are measured and shown in Fig. 2c. A significant augment in G band indicates increase in sp2 carbon content that reveals the pre-CDs undergone modification of HBT, a same conclusion to FT-IR [38]. Then the 1H NMR are carried out shown in Fig. 2d. The chemical shift values of pre-CDs are mainly concentrated near the high field of 1–4 ppm (sp3 CH/-NH/-NH2) in line with Raman spectral data. The peaks of V-CDs show near 6–9 ppm after HBT modification, which belongs to the chemical shift value of hydrogen on the benzene ring. Simultaneously, peaks of the amino group of 2.7–3.7 ppm on pre-CDs disappears, which indicates that HBT-CHO reacts with -NH2 on pre-CDs, and V-CDs is obtained after reduction process [39]. In summary, a CDs model covered by HBT is built as shown in Fig. 1e.

The fluorescence property of pre-CDs and V-CDs is very important parameters for determining the test condition and discussing the mechanism. First, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the materials in different solution environments were tested. As shown in Fig. 3a, HBT shows the property of emission changing with viscosity. Its emission wavelength in a low-viscosity system is 395 nm, a dark purple light that is hard to see with the naked eye, but when viscosity increases, its emission wavelength redshifts to 520 nm and emits bright green fluorescence, which shows the AIE nature of HBT. For HBT-CHO, its emission does not change with viscosity, the excitation wavelength is at 350 nm and the emission wavelength is around 530 nm (Fig. S3a). The fluorescence properties of pre-CDs as shown in Figs. 3b and S3b, the optimal excitation wavelength of pre-CDs is around 360 nm. Emission wavelength is red-shifted about 8 nm in water compared to ethanol and exhibits emission-dependent excitation properties. The optimal excitation wavelength of the product V-CDs is about 370 nm. The emission wavelength of V-CDs in water is approximately red-shifted 10 nm than in ethanol and exists a characteristic of excitation-dependent emission (Figs. 3c and S3c) [43]. Its characteristic of excitation-dependent emission is obvious in ethanol and almost absent in water. The reason for this may be that HBT molecules modified on the surface of pre-CDs are affected by the water environment, because HBT is insoluble in water. This feature is not shown in the glycerol solution, but the fluorescence turns to green (λex/em = 340/515 nm in glycerol, Fig. 3d). Three conclusions can be drawn from the discussion of the basic fluorescence properties: a) HBT has viscosity response capability; b) V-CDs exhibit both pre-CDs and HBT fluorescence properties; c) The AIE effect of V-CDs will not be forced to display in water.

The influence of pH was studied. In different pH buffers (3–11), V-CDs is quenched under strong acidic conditions (pH < 6) and enhanced at pH 6–8 and red-shifted at pH 9–11 (Fig. 3f). The intensity value of peaks shows that the fluorescence of V-CDs is the strongest at pH about 8 (Fig. S3d). The effect of different solutions on the fluorescence of V-CDs is carried out as shown in Fig. 3e. The property of pre-CDs incompatible with organic solvents is changed to some extent after modified by HBT but V-CDs still showed weak fluorescence in cyclohexane, acetone, ethyl acetate, tetrahydrofuran, dichloromethane, and petroleum ether. In stronger polar solvents such as DMSO, DMF, water and so on, V-CDs shows strong fluorescence and a strong excitation-independent emission in glycerol as described above specially. Then, different proportions solutions of ethanol and glycerin are used to simulate the solution system with increasing viscosity. As shown in Fig. 3g, the peak value at about 440 nm decreases continuously and the peak value at about 515 nm increases continuously, and the color of the solution system changes from blue to green [44]. The change in color coordinates is clearly shown in the CIE diagram (Fig. 1h). Based on the Forster-Hoffmann equation, corresponding relationship between fluorescence intensity and viscosity is obtained by measuring the viscosity of different ratios of ethanol and glycerol solutions. As shown in Fig. S4, the linear relationship is more obvious in the range of 107–945 cp, which indicated that V-CDs are more sensitive in high viscosity. On the other hand, this process can be quickly achieved by adding ethanol to glycerol (Supplementary Video). As shown in Fig. S5, the effect of temperature on fluorescence of V-CDs was carried out. The fluorescence intensity is decreased with the increase of temperature and the ability in high temperature be affected, but these effects is minuscule, especially when it is applied to biological systems. In these experiments, the fluorescence properties and AIE properties were mainly discussed. These conclusions indicate that V-CDs have advantages in fluorescence properties, adaptation range, visualization, etc.

V-CDs show obvious fluorescence process from blue to green by the change of viscosity, which is the result of alternating action of pre-CDs with ACQ and HBT with AIE. Comparing the fluorescence spectra of pre-CDs, HBT, and V-CDs (Fig. 4a), it can be found that the spectrum of V-CDs overlaps with the fluorescence spectrum of pre-CDs. In the high-viscosity solution, on the contrary, the spectrum of V-CDs overlaps the fluorescence spectrum of HBT. In the UV–vis spectrum, the absorption peaks of HBT, HBT-CHO, and V-CDs almost overlap, whether in a low viscosity solution (ethanol, Fig. 4b) or a high viscosity solution (Glycerol, Fig. 4b), which makes them emit optimal fluorescence under same maximum absorption wavelength. The alternating fluorescence action of pre-CDs and HBT is based on the above facts. In low viscosity, the fluorescence of V-CDs is excited by the internal pre-CDs, while HBT molecules do not emit obvious fluorescence due to the RIR effect. With the increase of environmental viscosity, the AIE effect of HBT molecules increases and leads to continuous enhancement of green fluorescence. The decrease in blue fluorescence may be caused by two reasons. Most of the excitation light energy is absorbed by HBT. Another reason may be the spatial confinement that causes the HBT molecule to approach pre-CDs, which makes the electrons in the excited state of the pre-CDs to be directly transferred to the HBT molecule, thus failing to return to its ground state leading to fluorescence quenching of pre-CDs. In order to show the influence of spatial limits on V-CDs, hydrogel swelling experiments was carried out. As the water absorption time (0–270 min) gel fluorescence changes from green to blue, which indicates that water molecules make the space of gel inside increases and gradually restore rotate freely state of HBT molecules, eventually making blue-fluorescent release (Fig. 4d). The edge of gel particles is blue and the center is green during the swelling process, indicating that water enters the interior from the surface, and the swelling also begins from the surface. By measuring the fluorescence spectra of the gel as shown in Fig. 4e, the peaks position is the same as solution. The side length of the gel doubled after swelling, and this high swelling rate is also a prerequisite for V-CDs to function (Fig. 4f). Finally, a hypothetical model of V-CDs fluorescence mechanism is proposed in Fig. 4g. Viscosity can be regarded as a converter of excitation light. By restricting the rotation of a single bond, the AIE effect of HBT molecules can be turned off or on, which leads to the fluorescent “blue-green” changes.

Through the discussion of the above experiments, the fluorescence properties and mechanisms of V-CDs have been summarized and basically complete. V-CDs have many excellent properties, such as being able to function in a variety of solutions, being able to work under complex conditions (gels), and having obvious visual differences. The source of these advantages is the multiple complementary functions manifesting in one substance. These complementary functions include AIE vs ACQ, hydrophilic vs hydrophobic. In addition, the drug activity vs biocompatibility will be discussed in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of VEGF by V-CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Before the staining of cells by V-CDs, MTT assay was carried out (Fig. S6). We treated MDA-MB-231 and fibroblast cells with different concentrations of V-CDs and observed the survival of cells after 12 h and 24 h. The results show that the cancerous cell viability decreased with the concentration of V-CDs and prolonged time and fibroblast is less affected. This experiment indicates that V-CDs can selectively affect cancerous cells because cancerous cells are more VEGF-dependent. Then, we performed a colocalization experiment of V-CDs. The probe was co-cultured in MDA-MB-231 cells stained with ZO-1 antibody (cell membrane localization) and DAPI staining (nuclear localization) to determine the presence of V-CDs in cells. As shown in Fig. S7, blue fluorescence exists in the cytoplasm and nucleus surrounded by red fluorescence, which indicates that V-CDs can enter the cells. Cells apoptosis is accompanied by changes in intracellular viscosity [45,46], so co-culture of Taxol with V-CDs is performed. The color of the cells changes from blue to green after adding chemotherapy drugs, which indicates that the intracellular viscosity increases. Afterward, different concentration Taxol was used to acquire different levels of apoptosis (Fig. 5a). Above experiments indicate that V-CDs can enter the interior of cells and exert its fluorescent properties, tracking the chemotherapy process and apoptosis degree of the cells.

In the previous literature, we found that HBT molecules have the ability of bind to VEGFR2, an important receptor for VEGF in the expression of VEGF signaling pathway. Considering the docking simulation of the HBT molecule for VEGFR2, we used ethyl-HBT (Fig. 5b) instead of V-CDs to dock with VEGFR2 and obtained two reasonable models (Figs. 5c and S8), one of which showed that the imine on HBT forms a hydrogen bond with the amino group on ASN923 in VEGFR2, on the other hand, hydroxyl group and the secondary amine in HBT forms hydrogen bonds with LEU840 in VEGFR2 (Fig. 5c). The another shows the imine in HBT, the hydrogen on the hydroxyl group, and the secondary amine form hydrogen bonds with LEU840 in VEGFR2 (Fig. S8). In addition, the plane structure of HBT can be embedded in the pore of proteins. Therefore, it can be concluded that the combination of V-CDs and VEGFR2 is possible, thereby affecting the expression of VEGF and blocking the entire pathway.

In order to prove the inhibitory effect of V-CDs on VEGF, fluorescent immunoprotein experiments were performed. The nuclei stained by DAPI can locate cancerous cells and the fluorescence antibody protein of VEGF can reflect the concentration of VEGF through the intensity of red fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 5c, the expression of VEGF is related to the concentration of V-CDs. The red fluorescence is decreased as the concentration of V-CDs increases, indicating that V-CDs exhibits the function of inhibiting VEGF. The effect is more obvious with the extension of time (12 h at Fig. S9 and 24 h at Fig. 5d). The inhibitory effect of V-CDs on VEGF reached good results at 100 μg/mL (Fig. 5e). In addition, it can be shown in MTT experiment that the effect of V-CDs on apoptotic cancerous cells is not obvious, so combined anti-cancer experiments are needed for rapid apoptosis of cancerous cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The combination anticancer of V-CDs and taxol
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the effect that the role of V-CDs in anti-cancer process, Taxol was introduced as an adjuvant chemotherapeutic agent. As shown in Fig. 6a, the shade of green fluorescence indicates the degree of cell death after the addition of these two substances. The effect of combined administration is significantly better than that of single administration group, and the result after 24 h (Fig. 6a) is better than the result after 12 h (Fig. S10). Comparing the blank group with the combined administration group, the apoptotic effect is improved by about 14 times (Fig. 6b). Then, the Western blotting experiment was carried out for simultaneously observing the expression of VEGF and Caspase-3 and further proof of the anti-VEGF effect of V-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6c, the VEGF imprints gradually become shallow. The expression of Caspase-3 is not obvious in the presence of V-CDs alone, and the effect of Taxol is obvious, and the combination group is much more effective than the alone. After quantification, express of VEGF has dropped 3.45-fold and Caspase-3 increased 6.68-fold (Fig. 6d). In summary, the expression of VEGF is related to the content of V-CDs, and it will cause certain apoptosis of cancerous cells. Moreover, it works better when combined with Taxol. Therefore, for the excessive proliferation of cancerous cells, the combined anti-cancer regimen of V-CDs and Taxol is effective and feasible in vitro.

The phase that V-CDs worked cooperatively with Taxol in vitro have been completed. Next, the combined therapeutic potential of V-CDs in vivo is preliminary analyzed by intravenous administration. Four groups of MDA-MB-231 tumor-bearing BALB/c nude mice were randomly selected as: (a) control, (b) V-CDs, (c) Taxol, and (d) V-CDs + Taxol, through 20 days of cultivation. By recording the body weight of the mice during treatment, no significant difference between them is found, and then the effect of V-CDs for the survival of the mice is negligible (Fig. S11, a and b). In the process of recording tumor volume and weight (Fig. S10c-e), this indicator shows the same conclusion as the WB experiment, that is, the tumor can be inhibited after administration, and combined administration is better than single administration. As shown in Fig. S11f, the inhibition of tumor mass of the three experimental groups was 13.64%, 29.44%, and 68.83%, respectively. For visualizing the anti-cancer effects, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) were used to stain the cancerous tissue cells as shown in Fig. S11g–j. By comparing experimental groups and the control group, local destruction of the tumor can be observed in all three experiments, which indicates that V-CDs can exert anti-cancer effects in vivo. In the co-administered group, some changes in the state of the cells can be more obviously observed like the necrosis of cells increased and the condensation of nuclear, which preliminary analysis indicate that the combination treatment of V-CDs and Taxol can inhibit and kill the growth of cancerous tissues on a large scale [47].

In summary, a viscosity-dependent V-CDs for visual anti-cancer process is synthesized and researched. It has the property of colortransform with viscosity and can be applied to a multi-water environment system, like hydrogels and organisms. In addition, it can effectively inhibit the production of VEGF and achieves anti-cancer effects by combining with Taxol. Thus, we visualized the process of apoptosis and reached anti-cancer effects. The expression of VEGF was reduced by 3.45 times, the expression of Caspase-3 was increased by 6.68 times, and the tumor mass was decreased by 68.83%. Moreover, in this article, the complementarity of various functions by modifying is achieved to synergistically perform their respective functions, including AIE vs ACQ, hydrophilic vs hydrophobic, and drug activity vs biocompatibility. The concept of modifying complementary functions has certain significance for the synthesis and application of CDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678409, 51638011, 51578375, 81801808), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology of China (19JCYBJC19800, 18JCYBJC87500, 15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507), Harbin Medical University Cancer Hospital Haiyan Foundation (JJZD2018-02), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in Tianjin University (TD13-5042).

B 4 (2016) 1968–1977. https://doi.org/10.1039/C5TB02476E.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quercetin-modified metal–organic frameworks for dual sensitization of radiotherapy in tumor tissues by inhibiting the carbonic anhydrase IX

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Ma, Y. Liu, Q. Wu, L. Luo, Y. Cui, X. Wang, X. Chen, L. Tan, X. Meng, Quercetin-Modified Metal–Organic Frameworks for Dual Sensitization of Radiotherapy in Tumor Tissues by Inhibiting the Carbonic Anhydrase IX, ACS Nano 13 (2019) 4209–4219. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.8b09221.
